label: Clustering
definition: The unsupervised learning task of grouping data points so that items within a group are more similar to each other than to items in other groups, discovering structure in data that carries no labels.

body.text: Clustering is the unsupervised learning task of partitioning a dataset into groups of similar items without any labels to guide the grouping. Where Supervised Learning is told the right answer for each example, clustering is given only the raw points and must invent the groups itself, using nothing but the geometry of the data. A good clustering places points that are close together, by some chosen measure of similarity, into the same cluster, and points that are far apart into different clusters. The result is a discovered structure: market segments hidden in customer data, topics latent in a pile of documents, cell types in a tissue sample, none of them specified in advance.

Clustering matters because labels are scarce and expensive, while raw data is abundant, so the ability to find structure without supervision is enormously useful. It serves as exploratory analysis that reveals what natural categories a dataset even contains, as a preprocessing step that compresses many points into a handful of representative groups, and as a component of larger systems, from anomaly detection (a point in no dense cluster is suspicious) to recommendation (users in the same cluster like similar things). It is the canonical example, alongside dimensionality reduction, of what Unsupervised Learning can do.

The methods differ chiefly in how they define a cluster, which is the crucial conceptual point. Centroid-based methods such as K-Means represent each cluster by a central point and assign every item to its nearest center, which is fast but assumes roughly spherical, equally sized groups and requires the number of clusters to be fixed in advance. Hierarchical clustering instead builds a tree of nested groupings, merging the closest pairs step by step, which needs no preset count and yields a dendrogram showing structure at every scale. Density-based methods such as DBSCAN define clusters as dense regions separated by sparse ones, which lets them find clusters of arbitrary shape and label sparse points as noise. There is no single correct method, because "similar" means different things for different data.

The genuinely hard part of clustering is that the task is ill-posed: without labels there is no ground truth, so there is no unambiguous way to say a clustering is right. The number of clusters is usually unknown and must be guessed or inferred. The notion of distance is a modeling choice that quietly determines the answer, and raw features often need scaling or projection (frequently via Principal Component Analysis) before distance is meaningful at all. Evaluation falls back on internal measures of how tight and well-separated the clusters are, such as the silhouette score, or on external agreement with held-out labels when any exist, but both are imperfect proxies for a goal that is fundamentally subjective.

Clustering sits at the center of unsupervised learning and connects outward in several directions. It is closely tied to the Latent Space view of data, since clusters are often best found not in the raw coordinates but in a learned, lower-dimensional representation where the meaningful groups separate cleanly. It complements Semi-Supervised Learning, where a few labels are spread across discovered clusters to label the rest cheaply. And it stands as the practical counterpart to classification: both carve data into groups, but classification is handed the groups as labels to reproduce, whereas clustering must discover the groups in the first place.
